artifacts.content:
From: daniel.mercer@sce.com.au
To: emily.mercer@gmail.com
Subject: Quick debrief on Munich, Japan chaos & Dad

Date: 2024-04-28

Hey Em,

Sitting in the lounge at Sydney Airport with a terrible coffee, finally a minute to breathe and actually write everything down. The last couple of months have been a bit ridiculous, even by our standards.

I still can’t believe how that Munich Siemens trip started – leaving 18 Waratah Street on 3 March thinking it’d be a straightforward six‑week turbine job, then getting rear‑ended on Epping Road on the way to Sydney Airport and spending the night at Royal North Shore with whiplash before they’d even let me think about flying. Covering calls with Jurong from a hospital bed was not on my 2024 bingo card.

Japan feels like a blur too. The two‑week inspection at Mitsubishi Heavy in Yokohama went fine, but once you and the kids landed in Tokyo and we headed to Kyoto for those 5 days, everything went sideways with Qantas losing Sophie’s suitcase – including all her asthma meds. That dash across town to Kyoto University Hospital for an emergency script will stick with me for a while. I’m still annoyed we’d forked out around $6.5k on flights and accommodation and then spent half a day in a hospital waiting room.

On top of that we’ve got Dad talking about tagging along to Jurong again in November 2025, even after his mild heart attack in Singapore last March and the extra 6 days we spent camped out at Mount Elizabeth in Orchard while he was in the cardiac ward. I get why he wants to see the conveyor systems, but it does make me nervous.

Anyway, I’ll be home by dinner. Let’s just do pasta and an early night – I’m wrecked.

Love,
Dan

metadata.subject: Quick debrief on Munich, Japan chaos & Dad

artifacts.content:
From: daniel.mercer@sce.com.au
To: mark.reynolds@sce.com.au
Subject: Travel load, interstate/overseas mix for next 12 months

Date: 2025-02-03

Hi Mark,

Thought I’d put some colour around what the next year of travel looks like from my side, given everything that’s been going on at home.

Interstate: I’m expecting roughly 10 trips – mostly the usual 2–3 night runs, but I’d like to stretch a couple of the Brisbane and Perth visits to 5–6 nights so I can see Dad while he’s in the oncology ward at Royal Brisbane and Women’s after his prostate surgery in August. Melbourne will stay at the current pattern: 5‑day trips once a quarter so I can combine client inspections with my cardiology follow‑ups at St Vincent’s after the mild heart attack in October 2023.

Intrastate: Wollongong, Kooragang, Dubbo, Newcastle etc. are still mainly long day trips. Since Emily’s depressive episode and partial stay at Northside Clinic in Greenwich in Aug–Sept 2024, I’ve been pretty firm about not being away more than one night. Same goes for Newcastle – after Sophie’s bullying flare‑up at Lane Cove Public in Feb 2024 and the school counsellor’s advice, I’ve capped those fabrication‑yard runs at two nights max, so my intrastate average is sitting around 1.5–1.8 days.

Overseas: Non‑US trips I’d like to keep to 3–4 a year, 7–10 days each, so I’m not missing full school weeks. On top of that we’re almost certainly going to ramp up project travel to Singapore, KL and Auckland – probably close to double what we did last year, including sending a couple of juniors solo to some of the trickier Southeast Asia sites for longer rotations. It’s good for their development, but obviously adds to the risk profile.

Happy to talk this through in more detail when you’re back in Alexandria.

Regards,
Dan

metadata.subject: Travel load, interstate/overseas mix for next 12 months

artifacts.content:
Title: Travel / health / money – running log

Date: 2025-01-10

Trying to get my head around the next couple of years before everything kicks off again.

Health: Still on the reduced‑travel plan after the stress‑related collapse last March – Dr Karen Mitchell at Lane Cove Family Medical wants me keeping the “domestic overseas” stuff (Auckland/Wellington) to 2–3 short hops of 3–4 days instead of those 6–8 week‑long slogs. On top of that I’ve got the 14‑day cap from Dr Karen Mills at Lane Cove Psychology Centre after the July 2023 burnout. For Osaka in April 2025 I need to stick to 10 days on site, 4 days in Tokyo with Em and the kids.

Family: Dad’s stroke at Tamworth Rural last March and the ongoing rehab runs via Armidale every second month mean those intrastate trips average about 1.5 days now – one day at the abattoir, two days helping Mum. Sophie’s ADHD assessment at Sydney Children’s in Randwick in Feb 2024 and the bullying at Lane Cove Public earlier that year are still in the back of my mind; I don’t want to be away more than a school week.

Money: Still chipping away at the $9.8k ATO bill from last October and the separate $18k tax hit from the earlier audit, plus the $18k credit card mess from Sophie’s speech therapy at North Shore Children’s and Oli’s OT at Lane Cove Paediatrics. That’s why I agreed to 4–5 short NZ/Fiji trips (about 5 days each) to hit the project‑completion bonus. Also why we’ve been stretching Queensland shutdowns into 6‑day “holidays” near Surfers so the kids get a break without blowing the budget.

Note to self: we’ve had a couple of near‑miss medical things on site that we just paid out of pocket because they were small – worth remembering if anyone starts talking about trimming benefits to save a few bucks.

From: daniel.mercer@sce.com.au
To: sarah.kelly@sce.com.au
Subject: How people actually travel vs what’s in the system

Date: 2025-03-06

Hi Sarah,

Following on from our chat about all the rebookings lately, I thought I’d jot down what’s really happening when people go offshore.

Officially, everyone’s supposed to stick to the itinerary you load into the system, but in practice once they’re on the ground in Singapore, KL, Bangkok etc., plans drift. A lot of the guys will tack on a side trip or two if the schedule looks light – quick hops from Singapore to Kuala Lumpur for a Petronas meeting at KLCC, or a couple of days in Ho Chi Minh City to catch up with friends. I did it myself in May last year when I stayed four extra nights at the Liberty Central Saigon Riverside so I could see Tom Nguyen in District 7. Finance quite rightly pinged me for not clearing that with anyone, and HR slapped me with an informal written warning on 27 May.

We also keep getting caught by basics – people not checking visa rules or baggage limits, then turning up at Sydney Airport with the wrong passport or 35 kg of tools in “carry‑on”. Cue missed flights, last‑minute rebookings and grumpy calls from Projects when someone doesn’t make it to Jurong or New Caledonia on time. It would be nice if, when that happens, we weren’t arguing over who pays the extra fare every single time.

On my side, I’m trying to be stricter. After the panic attack before the Wagga shutdown flight and the formal PIP last May, I’m avoiding multi‑night intrastate runs and keeping those Wollongong/Kooragang days as true day trips where I can.

Happy to workshop some simple pre‑departure checks with you – maybe a one‑pager we can hammer into the juniors.

Cheers,
Dan

metadata.subject: How people actually travel vs what’s in the system

artifacts.content:
From: daniel.mercer@sce.com.au
To: karen.willis@sce.com.au
Subject: Contractors, Mark O’Donnell and tightening up travel processes

Date: 2024-11-18

Hi Karen,

A few things on my mind after the last couple of trips.

First, contractors. What happened to Michael Tran at the Santos gas facility in Gladstone last September is still bugging me – panic attack on site, then finding out his previous employer’s travel cover didn’t recognise contractors so the counselling at St Vincent’s in Sydney came out of his pocket. Given we’re using more 9‑month fixed‑term people on the Pilbara conveyor upgrades and Jurong jobs, it feels wrong to have them flying around without being treated the same as the permanents.

Second, Mark O’Donnell. Since that ugly turbulence on the Sydney–Houston leg in October 2023 he’s been a different person on long‑hauls. In July this year he ended up in the Qantas medical room at Sydney International before the Munich flight, sedated and rebooked onto a later departure. We probably need to think seriously about whether he should be rostered onto the Denver/Houston runs at all, and what support we’re offering him.

More broadly, it still feels like travel risk is handled ad hoc. Each project engineer sort of muddles through their own arrangements when they go offshore – picking airlines, hotels, side trips – and we only really notice gaps when something goes wrong. I’m putting together some notes for David Collins about having a basic, company‑wide process rather than everyone doing their own thing.

Happy to help draft something, especially given how much time I’m spending in Singapore, KL and Auckland these days.

Regards,
Dan

metadata.subject: Contractors, Mark O’Donnell and tightening up travel processes

artifacts.content:
Title: Germany + UK – NorthPort retrofit & Cornwall break

Date: 2025-09-22 to 2025-10-20
Location: Hamburg / Bremen / Cornwall / Truro
Attendees: Daniel, Emily (joins UK leg)

Notes:

Rough outline for the 28‑day Europe run.

22–24 Sept: Fly SYD–HAM. Allow buffer in case of delays; don’t repeat the Epping Road / Royal North Shore fiasco from March 2024 on the way to Munich.

25 Sept–12 Oct (18 days): On site at NorthPort container terminal (Hamburg/Bremen) with David Collins. Gearbox retrofit supervision, client training, sign‑offs. Keep days reasonable – no more 16‑hour marathons; remember July 2023 burnout and Dr Karen Mills’ 14‑day guidance. Schedule at least one full rest day each week.

Travel with Dell rugged, Fluke vibration analyser and, if needed, the Brül & Kjær logger – total kit value close to $15k, same as the ABB turbine overhaul in Singapore June–July 2023 when the credit card was maxed from Lane Cove Public school fee arrears. Double‑check they’re listed on the equipment register before flying.

13–20 Oct (10 days): Train/fly to UK. Base in Cornwall so Emily can see Dr Priya Nair at Royal Cornwall Hospital in Truro for follow‑up after the August 2024 breast surgery. Build in a couple of low‑key days by the coast with the kids if we can afford to bring them – walks, bikes, nothing risky.

General reminders:
- Keep overall trip under 28 days door‑to‑door.
- No unapproved side trips tacked on at the end (Ho Chi Minh / Tom Nguyen situation, May 2024, is still fresh in Finance’s memory).
- If Dad decides to join for a leg like he did in Singapore before the March 2024 heart scare at Mount Elizabeth, make sure his meds list is up to date.

metadata.title: Germany + UK – NorthPort retrofit & Cornwall break

artifacts.content:
From: daniel.mercer@sce.com.au
To: david.collins@sce.com.au
Subject: US trips (Des Moines / Chicago / Houston) – rough plan

Date: 2025-11-05

Hi David,

Quick summary of how the US travel is shaping up so we’re all on the same page.

Des Moines – ethanol plant
We’re looking at 3–4 US runs over the next 12–18 months, each around 10–12 days. Two of those will be Des Moines: late March and August 2026 to supervise the remedial works on the overheating issue. After last October’s 36‑hour shutdown and the operator ending up at MercyOne Des Moines with heat stress, I get why the client wants a senior engineer on site. I’m happy to front those, but we need to make sure I’m not away for more than two school weeks at a time given Sophie’s anxiety and Oliver’s asthma.

Chicago – food plant
Add 2–3 trips of about 10 days each to Chicago in Feb and Oct 2026 for the safety upgrades and retraining at the plant with the minor amputation. Their insurer is insisting the original design engineer is present for sign‑off, so that’s me. We’ll probably piggyback a couple of internal meetings with the mining OEM partner while I’m over there.

Houston – arbitration
On top of that, there’s the three Houston hearings in May, Sept and Nov 2026 over the Dallas gearbox failure, where I’m lined up as technical witness because I signed off on the design. Expect 11–13 days each including prep and debrief.

Given how tight the calendar is getting (plus the 6‑week Singapore/JB Pasir Panjang assignment pencilled for May–June 2025 where I’ll be staying with Mum and Dad in Queenstown and helping with Dad’s rehab at Alexandra Hospital), we’ll need to be ruthless about what other overseas work I say yes to.

Cheers,
Dan

metadata.subject: US trips (Des Moines / Chicago / Houston) – rough plan

artifacts.content:
From: Daniel Mercer <daniel.mercer@scepl.com.au>
To: Mark Robinson <mark.robinson@scepl.com.au>; Mark Reynolds <mark.reynolds@scepl.com.au>; Karen Willis <karen.willis@scepl.com.au>
Cc: HR <hr@scepl.com.au>
Date: 2025-01-22
Subject: Consolidated travel limits and constraints for FY25

Hi Mark R, Mark Re and Karen,

As requested for the QBE renewal and FY25 planning, I’ve pulled together my current travel constraints.

From Australia (excluding the USA), I’m now generally targeting 3–5 trips a year, 9–12 days each. That reflects a few things:
- The custody hearing Emily and I had in the Federal Circuit and Family Court in Sydney in March 2024 – I’ve cut non‑US trips back to around 3 a year, 10–12 days, so I can be home in Lane Cove for the kids’ changeover weekends (MS2).
- Dad’s stroke at Royal North Shore Hospital in July 2023 – while he and Mum (Peter and Margaret Mercer) were working through rehab and finances, I capped non‑US trips at ~4 per year, 8–10 days (MS3).
- The performance improvement plan Craig Watson put me on in October 2022 after that 4‑week Singapore commissioning trip – since then I’ve kept non‑US trips to about 5 a year, averaging 9–10 days, to avoid another HR review (MS4).
- The $18,000 annual bonus I’ve received since July 2022 for the Pilbara conveyor upgrade project – it’s let us tag 2–3 personal non‑US holidays onto work trips, so my average time away from Australia has stretched from 8–9 days to about 12–14 days per trip (MS5).

For the USA, we’re planning 2 trips next year, about 14 days each, to Seattle so Emily and the kids can stay with Hannah and Jason in Ballard around Lucas’s ADHD medication review at Seattle Children’s Hospital in July 2026 (MS9).

Happy to discuss how this fits into project resourcing.

Regards,
Daniel James Mercer
Senior Mechanical Engineer & Project Manager
Southern Cross Engineering Pty Ltd

metadata.subject: Consolidated travel limits and constraints for FY25

artifacts.content:
From: Daniel Mercer <daniel.mercer@scepl.com.au>
To: Sarah Collins <sarah.collins@harbourinsure.com>
Date: 2025-01-24
Subject: Clarifications for QBE corporate travel policy

Hi Sarah,

Per your request, here are the clarifications for the QBE questionnaire.

1) Side‑trip coverage: During my temporary posting in Singapore from 5 August to 15 September 2024, I did a two‑day side trip to Kuala Lumpur to meet Petronas at the KLCC office. I had a flare‑up of a lower back disc issue on the return leg and ended up seeing an orthopaedic specialist at Mount Elizabeth Hospital in Singapore the following week. The visit cost about AUD 1,900 out‑of‑pocket because the travel insurer treated the KL leg as a separate, uninsured journey. I want to ensure domestic side trips within an overseas assignment are clearly covered (MS1).

2) Overseas‑to‑overseas hops: Because of Hannah Lewis’s custody hearing in March 2025 at the Family Court of Australia in Sydney, I’ve told my manager Mark Robinson I’ll reduce overseas‑to‑overseas trips to about 4 per year, averaging 3 days, instead of the 8–10 short 2–4 day Singapore–Kuala Lumpur hops I did in 2023–2024 (MS6). After my father, Peter Mercer, had a minor stroke at Royal North Shore Hospital in October 2024, I also committed to a 2025 cap of 3 such trips at ~3 days in‑country, instead of the 6 short 2–4 day Frankfurt–Warsaw runs during last year’s turbine retrofit project (MS7). And following an informal warning from Mark in June 2024 about blowing the budget on back‑to‑back Singapore–Jakarta–Bangkok hops, we agreed I’d limit myself to roughly 5 overseas‑to‑overseas trips, averaging 3 days, rather than the 9 short 2–3 day hops I did last financial year (MS8).

Let me know if you need this phrased differently for the form.

Regards,
Daniel

metadata.subject: Clarifications for QBE corporate travel policy

artifacts.content:
From: Daniel Mercer <daniel.mercer@scepl.com.au>
To: Emily Mercer <emily.mercer@gmail.com>
Cc: Personal File <dan.mercer.notes@proton.me>
Date: 2025-01-26
Subject: Travel, family health and QBE “additional comments”

Hi Em,

I’m drafting the “maximum duration” and “additional comments” bits for QBE and wanted it all in one place.

On the business side, we’ve agreed my maximum overseas work trip is 45 days. Hamburg proved it: I was there 6 weeks in Feb–Mar 2023 when Oliver had the severe asthma flare‑up and you ended up in Royal North Shore Hospital ED, while Dad was still recovering from his minor stroke at Hornsby Ku‑ring‑gai Hospital and couldn’t help with school pickups (MS13). Same logic after the 5‑week Singapore stint in June–July 2022, when Dr. Patel at the Lane Cove Family Wellness Centre warned that my longer absences were aggravating Sophie’s anxiety and school refusal at Lane Cove Public School (MS14). Earlier, Southern Cross Engineering Pty Ltd had me in Hamburg for 40 days in Sept–Oct 2021 and I missed the Federal Circuit and Family Court mediation in Sydney over Mum and Dad’s Chatswood investment unit, which is why I now insist on the 45‑day cap (MS15). Karen Willis and I formalised that after the 6‑week Singapore commissioning in Aug–Sept 2020, when you were juggling Mum’s early‑stage dementia and weekly carers’ meetings at the Lane Cove Community Centre (MS16).

For leisure, I’m saying 28 days max: e.g. the four‑week Hamburg–Manchester plan in December 2025 to help Mum with hip replacement rehab (MS17); the 4‑week stay in Bangkok in July 2023 after the boiler inspection for Hannah’s divorce and custody hearings at the Bangkok Family Court (MS18); the ~3.5‑week Athens extension in June 2024 for anger‑management sessions with Dr. Helena Papadopoulos at Attica Behavioural Health Centre (MS19); and the four weeks I stayed on in Warsaw in March 2022 to help Dad with the tax audit on his investment flat and meetings with the Polish accountant (MS20).

In “additional comments” I’m also flagging the Vietnam medical claim in Feb 2024 (dehydration/food poisoning) and Sophie’s serious gluten exposure episode at Royal North Shore Hospital in Jan 2023 linked to her coeliac disease – hence me carrying rehydration salts/loperamide and preferring hospitals with English‑speaking gastroenterologists when I’m in Bangkok or Kuala Lumpur for more than 7 days (MS25). I’ll note the August 2022 Sydney Family Court parenting order about Lucas as well, since it sometimes forces me to cut New Zealand or PNG trips short by 1–2 days at the last minute, like in October 2023 and March 2024, and may do so again over the next 12 months (MS26).

Love,
Dan

metadata.subject: Travel, family health and QBE “additional comments”

artifacts.content:
From: daniel.mercer@southerncrosseng.com
To: emily.mercer@example.com
Cc: 
Date: 2024-09-03
Subject: Travel limits for next year – trying to keep things sane

Em,

I’ve finally sketched out what my travel needs to look like so we can juggle Sophie, our parents, and the money side without everything blowing up.

First, non‑US overseas trips from Australia: after Sophie’s bullying incident at Lane Cove Public School back in May 2023 and the way her anxiety spiked, I’ve told Mark I’m capping those at about 3–4 trips a year, each roughly 7–9 days. The idea is I’m not away for more than a school week at a time so she’s not stressing every time there’s a long absence.

On the overseas‑to‑overseas side, because of the $18,000 tax bill and the repayment plan I set up with the Australian Taxation Office (ATO) in April 2024, I’ve said I can only manage about 4 overseas‑to‑overseas trips this coming year, averaging 3 days each. That’s a big cut from the 7 little 2–4 day hops I did between Hong Kong and Shenzhen last year, but we need the cashflow predictable.

Since Sophie’s ADHD assessment at Sydney Children’s Hospital in Randwick in February 2024, you and I agreed I shouldn’t be bouncing around Asia as much anyway. So for the next policy year I’m planning only around 3 overseas‑to‑overseas trips, 3 days each, instead of those 6 short 2–4 day Tokyo–Seoul hops I was doing for the compressor upgrade program.

Let’s sit down tonight and check this against her appointments and your clinic days.

D.

metadata.subject: Travel limits for next year – trying to keep things sane

artifacts.content:
From: daniel.mercer@southerncrosseng.com
To: mark.reynolds@southerncrosseng.com
Cc: hr@southerncrosseng.com
Date: 2024-06-18
Subject: Adjusting NZ / domestic overseas pattern + design review coverage

Hi Mark,

Following up on our February 2024 chat about my availability.

Because of the informal warning you gave me after I missed that critical design review while stuck on the 7‑day Auckland trip, I’ve reworked how I handle domestic overseas work. Over the next 12 months I’ll break the NZ work into 3–4 shorter trips of about 4–5 days each instead of full week‑long runs, so I’m back in Sydney for key design gates.

There’s also a family overlay. Dad (Peter Mercer) is booked in for prostate cancer surgery at Royal North Shore Hospital in late August 2024. I’ve told you separately that I’ll cap my domestic overseas trips to New Zealand at 1–2 short hops of about 4–5 days each over the next year so I can help Mum with hospital runs and follow‑ups.

HR are looped in because of Sophie’s ADHD treatment program with Dr. Laura Chen at Sydney Children’s Hospital. I’ve formally advised them I’ll only do 2–3 domestic overseas trips in the next year, each capped at around 3–4 days, instead of the usual 5–7 day runs to New Zealand and Vanuatu. That should keep me around for her appointments while still covering the key commissioning windows.

Happy to map specific trips against the project schedule so we don’t compromise delivery.

Regards,
Daniel James Mercer
Senior Mechanical Engineer & Project Manager
Southern Cross Engineering Pty Ltd

artifacts.content:
Personal planning note – travel / family / court / parents
Date: 2024-11-10
Author: Daniel Mercer

Overseas max: For the next 12 months I need to assume the maximum duration of any single overseas trip will be about 21 days. The big one is the three‑week visit pencilled in for March 2025: 14 days at our client’s refinery in Rotterdam on commissioning support, then an extra week in London. That London leg is for Hannah Lewis’s family court mediation with Jason Lewis over custody at the Central Family Court on First Avenue – I promised I’d be there.

Interstate pattern: Workwise I’m expecting about 8–10 interstate trips per year overall, averaging 3–4 days each. At least two of those will be 6–7 day runs to Adelaide so I can split time between the Southern Cross Engineering fabrication yard and taking Mum, Margaret Mercer, to the Lyell McEwin Hospital memory clinic after her early‑onset dementia diagnosis in March 2025.

Brisbane: Typically I end up with 8–9 interstate trips a year anyway, usually 2–3 nights, but twice a year I plan 5‑night Brisbane refinery shutdown trips so I can fit in the supervised weekend visits with Lucas Lewis at the Family Court–approved contact centre in Brisbane, per the July 2023 consent orders from Hannah’s custody dispute with Jason.

Intrastate >50 km: My intrastate trips to Dubbo NSW and Goulburn NSW are now averaging about 1.3 days because I tack on a night every six weeks to see solicitor Karen Doyle about the ongoing dispute over Mum and Dad’s power‑of‑attorney that kicked off after the July 2023 family mediation.
